vulnerability.title: Record Events that Modify the System's Discretionary Access Controls - fchmodat
vulnerability.description: At a minimum, the audit system should collect file permission changes for all users and root. If the auditd daemon is configured to use the augenrules program to read audit rules during daemon startup (the default), add the following line to a file with suffix .rules in the directory /etc/audit/rules.d: -a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S fchmodat -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -F key=perm_mod If the system is 64 bit then also add the following line: -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S fchmodat -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -F key=perm_mod If the auditd daemon is configured to use the auditctl utility to read audit rules during daemon startup, add the following line to /etc/audit/audit.rules file: -a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S fchmodat -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -F key=perm_mod If the system is 64 bit then also add the following line: -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S fchmodat -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -F key=perm_mod
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q audit && rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then # First perform the remediation of the syscall rule # Retrieve hardware architecture of the underlying system [ "$(getconf LONG_BIT)" = "32" ] && RULE_ARCHS=("b32") || RULE_ARCHS=("b32" "b64") for ARCH in "${RULE_ARCHS[@]}" do ACTION_ARCH_FILTERS="-a always,exit -F arch=$ARCH" OTHER_FILTERS="" AUID_FILTERS="-F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset" SYSCALL="fchmodat" KEY="perm_mod" SYSCALL_GROUPING="chmod fchmod fchmodat" # Perform the remediation for both possible tools: 'auditctl' and 'augenrules' unset syscall_a unset syscall_grouping unset syscall_string unset syscall unset file_to_edit unset rule_to_edit unset rule_syscalls_to_edit unset other_string unset auid_string unset full_rule

triage_decision.reason: Adding audit rules for fchmodat is a standard configuration change that does not affect authentication, networking, or filesystem operation. It follows STIG hardening patterns for audit logging and is unlikely to cause lockout or system breakage.
triage_decision.estimated_impact: audit configuration change; potential misconfiguration leading to audit failure

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The plan will add the required audit rules but does not trigger a reload of auditd. The vulnerability will only be fully addressed after the audit daemon is restarted or the new rule file is explicitly reloaded.
concerns: Auditd does not automatically reload new rule files when they are added to /etc/audit/rules.d. The proposed plan assumes automatic reload, which is incorrect on most systems.
The rule file permissions and ownership are not explicitly set, which could lead to improper access control over the rule file.
suggested_improvements: After creating or updating /etc/audit/rules.d/perm_mod.rules, run either `systemctl restart auditd` or `auditctl -R /etc/audit/rules.d/perm_mod.rules` to apply the changes immediately.
Set file ownership to root and permissions to 0600 for /etc/audit/rules.d/perm_mod.rules to prevent tampering.
Optionally verify the system architecture before adding the b64 rule to avoid unnecessary rules on 32‑bit systems, though this is harmless.
